mappings.rationaleAI: The Almoravids' origins as a Saharan religious reform federation, conquest of Morocco under Yusuf ibn Tashfin, intervention in Spain against the Reconquista, and eventual decline before the Almohad revolt correspond precisely to B.4.c.i on Almoravid origins, conquests, and displacement.

The Mamluk Sultanate's overthrow of the Ayyubids in 1250, defeat of the Mongols at Ain Jalut (1260), expulsion of the last Crusader strongholds, and the Bahri-to-Burji transition directly correspond to B.4.b.ii on Mamluk resistance to Mongols, military institutions, and Sunni orthodoxy.

Venice's maritime empire and strategic outposts beyond Constantinople are inseparable from the Fourth Crusade's diversion to Constantinople (1202-04) and the fluctuating Venetian-Byzantine relations under the later Comneni covered in A.1.c.ii and A.1.c.iii.

Eleanor's participation in the Second Crusade and her dynastic connection to Richard the Lionheart, a principal leader of the Third Crusade, link directly to A.1.b on the Second and Third Crusades and their leading magnates.

Saladin's seizure of Jerusalem in 1187, the papal call to crusade, and the campaigns of Frederick Barbarossa, Philip of France, and Richard the Lionheart correspond precisely to A.1.b on the Third Crusade (1188-92), the crusader states to 1187, and the magnates of the Third Crusade.

The 1453 fall of Constantinople to Sultan Mehmet II, including advances in cannon technology and the end of the Byzantine Empire, maps directly to A.3.c (final Turkish assault and fall of Constantinople) and B.3.b.i (Ottoman conquest of Constantinople).

Melisende's rule of the Crusader Kingdom of Jerusalem from 1131, her political alliances, and administrative governance of the Frankish state illustrate the institutions of the First Kingdom and Christian colonization in the East covered in A.1.a.ii and A.1.b.

The Baltic Crusades represent the extension of crusading ideology beyond the Holy Land, with the Teutonic Order's state-building in Prussia after being thwarted in Jerusalem illustrating the survival and broader reach of the crusading spirit among Latin Christians (A.1.d).

The broad history of the Byzantine Empire, Constantinople as a center of classical learning, and the empire's thousand-year survival and fall provide essential context for A.1.c (the Comnenus-era empire and its relations with crusaders) and A.3 (the Palaeologus restoration through the 1453 fall).

Islamic civilization in Iberia spanning the Almoravid and Almohad periods, the Reconquista, and the Nasrid kingdom of Granada corresponds to B.4.c (Almoravid and Almohad empires in Spain) and B.4.d.i (the Nasrid kingdom of Granada and its cultural life).

The twelfth-century Prester John legend of a powerful Eastern Christian king poised to ally with Crusaders against Islam illustrates the ideological dimensions and persistent hopes that sustained the crusading spirit among Latin Christians (A.1.d.ii).

Genghis Khan's unification of the Mongol peoples and the devastating invasions his empire launched into eastern Europe and Southwest Asia correspond directly to B.2 (Mongolian invasions of the 13th century) and A.1.e (the Mongol invasion of Russia and establishment of Tatar rule).

The Volga Vikings' founding of trading settlements that became the nuclei of Kievan Rus provides essential background for A.1.e on the lands of Rus and the rise of new centers like Novgorod before the Mongol invasion.
Pilgrimage to Jerusalem connects to the Crusading movement and the broader relationship between Western Christendom and the Holy Land.
